content:
I open this thread to capsule more or less all DmaFence discussions so they are easy to find.

Note that I recently opened a new draft for DmaFence that took much previous design goals and feedback into account.

You must not allocate in a fence signalling section, which is often the case when trying to register a callback, for instance in JobQueue.

@Boris Brezillon has rightfully pointed out that my callback registration is still broken. An idea I had which might solve this seems largely identical with one that @Daniel Almeida had, too.

I suppose what Daniel presents looks like I good solution, but if folks like @Alice Ryhl @Gary Guo or @Danilo Krummrich could take a look, too, I think that would be good.

An ideal and ellegant solution in my mind would probably look ca. like that:

let cb = FenceCallback::new(data);
let mem = KBox::pin_init(cb)?;
…
fence.register_callback(mem);

I think I have an idea.

The thing with the code presented by and Daniel is that we would basically store the same information that the C backend already has another time, in another allocation, in Rust.

But this information is already stored deep down in the C dma_fence: 

struct dma_fence {
    union {
        spinlock_t *extern_lock;
        spinlock_t inline_lock;
    };
    const struct dma_fence_ops __rcu *ops;

    union {
        struct list_head cb_list;

There's a list of all callbacks that are waiting for this fence to be signalled. The fence knows which callbacks are registered and which are signalled already.

Now, if we were able to port the C backend so that the backend uses an XArray instead of a list, we might benefit twice. Once because the backend gets a bit faster since the xarray is more cache-friendly when iterating the callbacks.

Twice because it might be possible to replace Daniel's FenceCallbackRegistration with some sort of ID wrapper

struct FenceRegistration {
  fence: ARef<Fence>, // no new memory, does not need allocation
  id: usize,
}

whose id would be used to obtain the correct callback through the XArray in the C fence. And then we could do container_of, if we can derive what generic data a callback data contains

So we might end up with:

let cb_object = FenceCallback::new(data)?; // allocates

...

let registration = fence.register_callback(cb_object); // does not need allocation

If you'd drop your registration, you'd look into the C backend whether it's still there, if yes, you get it back and can drop it – if not, it's signaled already (ownership over data being transferred back to the driver).

Just had the idea a few mins ago. Not sure if I'm missing something, but it could be very clean because we'd rely on the C backend, who already has all the relevant information, doing the work? @Boris Brezillon
@Philipp Stanner fyi the reason why I am taking a bit to reply is because I am rebasing all that code on 7.0 and it’s taking a while
xarray for the cb list comes with the extra annoyance that the xarray might allocate, and C users are potentially in a path where certain kind of allocations might not be possible
by "the same information", you mean the fact we encode whether it's been called or not through an Option<T>, which we also know that from the drm_fence_callback object (list_empty vs !list_empty)?
I believe the Option<T> was here so your FenceCallbackRegistration can give up its inner T (with a ::take()), and pass it to the ::signaled() method.
Please no
Consume the registration instead
It’s way simpler than producing a &mut to call “take()”
I had the take approach at first, and it was hard to make lockdep happy
WDYM?

@Boris Brezillon The registration object, created through that closure, basically exists for the sole purpose of you being able to re-obtain your dma_fence_cb, to ask the dma_fence backend about the state.

So the problem we are trying to solve is: How can we access our callback again, without allocating something when registering it?

And my argument is that we already have the fence, so we could re-obtain it from the fence.
dma_fence_cb has to come from somewhere, so where would that be?
You mean where it is initially created?
where is dma_fence_cb living, yes

The Rust driver allocates it.
When registering it, memory ownership is given to the C backend, who keeps it in its cb_list

If Rust needs it again, you access it via the Fence -> dma_fence -> cb_list -> container_of
has to be embedded in some object, and you need to get back to some user-specific T
can you describe what this object containing dma_fence_cb looks like?
I mean, that's pretty much how @Daniel Almeida does it, isn't it? The FenceCallbackRegistration has a dma_fence_cb, plus an Option<T>

It would be the same as currently

pub struct FenceCb<T: FenceCbFunc> {
    /// C struct needed for the backend.
    #[pin]
    inner: Opaque<bindings::dma_fence_cb>,
    /// Driver data.
    #[pin]
    pub data: T,
}

The part I'm unsure about is whether we could make container_of work, IOW where to obtain the generic info from, considering that Fence cannot be generic
Well, not quite, Daniel's version keeps all the data stored in Rust and needs to allocate for T

this "get back to FenceCb from dma_fence_cb" works (it's basically what we do for DriverFenceData), it's not really the problem. I think this gets tricky if T is pinned, because then you can't take the value, and pass ownership to the ::signaled() method
as for Daniel's version allocating memory separately, I don't think that's true, is it?

It's the entire point, no?
You want some sort of handle returned by let handle = fence.register_callback(cb), so that you can deregister that cb again.
And that handle needs to be allocated, which is why an impl PinInit is returned
that's the FenceCallbackRegistration you're talking about?
Yes

okay, let's take it one field at a time:

  • dma_fence_cb, you can't really get rid of, it's what gets registered to the C object
  • Option<T> is just the size of T wrapped into an enum that encodes the state. So it's likely something like sizeof(T) + align(1bit, align_of(T)), since there's only two variants to encode (None and Some)
  • ARef<Fence>, this one we "could" get rid of, but it would become a mess in term of lifetime control and who owns who (like, how do you deregister, if the fence you registered to is gone, and callback has been consumed)
is the problem the overhead of Option?

just to be clear, what I mean is that the allocation of a FenceCallbackRegistration object is still one block that contains everything for the callback to run and for the registration object to be dropped before this callback has run

if you were to dissociate the "run" from the "unsubscribe" aspects, you'd need #DRM > DmaFence discussions @ 💬 for the callback execution side of things, plus another Registration object containing an opaque u32 ID plus the ARef<Fence>, so I'm not too sure we'd be saving memory. Am I missing something?

We're misunderstanding each other a bit. Let's agree on a few things first:

  • Our problem is that we must be able to deregister callbacks
  • If we didn't need that, we could design fence.register_callback() so that it does not allocate and would be 'done'
  • The pin_init_from_closure is his way to give the driver the ability to allocate when it deems it appropriate / without violating the fence contract

Right?

we must be able to deregister callbacks

Yes

If we didn't need that, we could design fence.register_callback() so that it does not allocate and would be 'done'

Not really, you need the dma_fence_cb+T to live somewhere, so this memory has to be allocated. Where/when it's allocated is an implementation decision, and that's, I think, what we were discussing yesterday.

The pin_init_from_closure is his way to give the driver the ability to allocate when it deems it appropriate / without violating the fence contract

You can allocate uninitialized memory and initialize the FenceCallbackRegistration late thanks to PinInit approach, though it's still something you'd have to do explicitly (KBox::new_uninit() at alloc time + KBox::?? to initialize in-place from the PinInit implementation? => that's a question for @Daniel Almeida or @Alice Ryhl )

Not really, you need the dma_fence_cb+T to live somewhere, so this memory has to be allocated.

Yes, of course. I never disputed that.
okay, you meant fence.register_callback() takes ownership of your FenceCallback object and the fence has to signal for this FenceCallback to be dropped

Yes.

The only thing I am arguing about is that a fence.deregister_callback(id) could be possible, where that function re-obtains the memory, for example by searching C's cb_list for the associated callback

Doesn't mean we have to aim for that, I'm just saying that I am considering that option

if producing the opaque ID doesn't involve allocating memory (so no xarray, I guess), yes. But my question then is, what do we save by not having the register_callback() return a Registration object that has this dma_fence_cb embedded (no search needed, and we don't have to change the C API).
We need to add some extra machinery to pin-init to reinitialize the Box in this case, but I already talked to Benno about it and he cleared it

We specifically need this, for the record:

fn drop_contents_pinned(this: Pin<Box<T>>) -> Box<MaybeUninit<T>>

From that we would reinitialize the box with

<Box<MaybeUninit<_>> as InPlaceWrite>::write_pin_init(self, init: impl PinInit<T, E>) -> Result<Pin<Box<T>>, E>

I think this might result in a more readable and a bit more logical API:

With the pin solution it is:

            let waker = DependencyWaker::new(jobq.clone(), job_ptr);
            let callback = FenceCbRegistration::new(&dep.fence, waker);

            let reg = KBox::pin_init(callback, GFP_KERNEL)?; // does the actual registering + allocation

Whereas with my idea it would be:

            let waker = DependencyWaker::new(jobq.clone(), job_ptr);
            let callback = FenceCbRegistration::new(waker)?; // does the allocation

            let reg = fence.register_callback(callback); // no alloc

I don't have super hard feelings about it, I think Daniel's solution does work.

But I believe that a callback registration is an operation on the fence.

Just like I would not expect a fence to get signalled like that: KBox::pin_init(f);, but like so: fence.signal()
The Box::write_pin_init() is something internal to FenceCbRegistration::new() BTW
I mean, it can even be a method of Fence
(though I'm not too sure we can get back to ARef<Fence> from &Fence, so that wouldn't really be a method taking &self...)
if it's just about ergonomics, we can probably find something
You can do ArcBorrow to get the ref I think
ArcBorrow if for Arc not ARef. But I see a bunch of From implementations for ARef<xxx> taking a ref and turning it into an ARef, so that's doable
yep, it's there => https://rust.docs.kernel.org/src/kernel/sync/aref.rs.html#158-164

OK.

I guess this solution is fine. Maybe we can pretty up the API a bit like that, but since it solves the problem, I'm OK with going with it

Boris Brezillon said:

KBox::new_uninit() at alloc time + KBox::?? to initialize in-place from the PinInit implementation?

KBox::new_uninit() gives you a KBox<MaybeUninit<T>>, since we have an InPlaceWrite<T> impl for Box<MaybeUninit<T>, A>, you can call write_pin_init() on a KBox<MaybeUninit<T>>. This call will consume the KBox<MaybeUninit<T>> and return a new Pin<KBox<T>> object.
^ needs drop_contents_pinned() as I said
It's not really that this is needed for the above to work, but you need this if you want to recycle a pinned box, yes.

I have updated my discussion merge request

@Daniel Almeida @Boris Brezillon

Important news:

  • Allow for pre-allocating fence callbacks (stolen from Daniel)
  • Allow for pre-allocating fences
  • Have fences refcount the fctx

I gave it a quick test run with my unit test.

The JobQueue in this branch is currently not operational; I want to fully port it next to the pre-allocated fences.

Please take a look and tell me whether you deem it ready enough for a wider discussion

I come to think that we will always need to wait a grace period before dropping a DriverFence

https://gitlab.freedesktop.org/pstanner/linux-drm-work/-/blob/jobqueue-dma-fence-v1/rust/kernel/dma_buf/dma_fence.rs?ref_type=heads#L663

What we achieved so far is that data is freed through call_rcu() so that no drop() needs to be called from atomic context, which is what @Danilo Krummrich was keen on.

We agreed that in case of core::memory::needs_drop(), we want to drop with call_rcu(), otherwise can drop immediately.

However, something that does not need drop could still be accessing data right now, even if data is just i32.

The memory the i32 resides in could not be freed thanks to refcounting. However, data could be accessed that Rust believes is dropped.

I'm not entirely sure whether that is illegal?
What we sort of have here is basically our own Revocable, where the fence's signalled-flag is the revoked boolean
@Boris Brezillon @Alice Ryhl @Gary Guo ^

However, something that does not need drop could still be accessing data right now, even if data is just i32.

You mean one of the dma_fence_ops::xxx implementation accessing data?
Aren't those calls protected with the rcu read lock?

Yes, that's what I mean.

They are protected by the RCU read lock, but our plan so far was to only defer dropping with RCU if data needs drop.

The question boils down to:
Is it illegal to access something that was dropped, no matter whether dropping it deallocates the backing memory?

If it is illegal, we would have to do 

if needs_drop(data) {
  call_rcu(drop(data))
} else {
   synchronize_rcu()
}
If needs_drop returns false, you do not need to drop it at all.
But what if someone does. Is that undefined behavior?
does what? I think needs_drop() tells you if there's anything to be executed on drop()
so if it returns false, the drop() implementation for this object is considered to be a NOP
Invoking drop on a type for which needs_drop() returns false is always a no-op.
Cool. Then we should be good I suppose

Philipp Stanner said:

What we achieved so far is that data is freed through call_rcu() so that no drop() needs to be called from atomic context, which is what @Danilo Krummrich was keen on.

I remember being told that call_rcu() closures may also be invoked in atomic context, so unless I misremember or have been told incorrectly, this doesn't help with the atomic context part.

Who said that? Paul?

If there were restrictions for what you can execute for rcu_callback_t, I'd expect that to be documented very explicitly, but can't see any such warning

By default call_rcu() callbacks run in softirq context (i.e. non-sleepable), but may be offloaded to a kthread. Obviously, for PREEMPT_RT it is always a kthread. For the "don't exectute in atomic context" part queue_rcu_work() is the correct tool.
I don't remember who said it, but I recall the part about softirq context as well, which is the reason for it being non-sleepable.
Then we need to rethink rcu::RcuBox, whose purpose would then probably be a pure defer free()
Or anything else that is non-sleepable.

Am I the only one who is afraid that our "don't trust the driver" approach comes at a higher and higher burden?

We would now delegate all drop operations for fence data to a workqueue, because we don't trust the driver to do non-sleepable things in Fence data.

We would, however, provide RcuBox in which we just as well would have to document "Driver, the data inside this box must not sleep when dropping. If it needs to sleep, use this other drop container that we wrote for DmaFence (originallry"

So in part, we just move the burden / documentation around
What about do a call_srcu?
SRCU callbacks are always invoked from workqueue
We could define a new trait AtomicDrop with a derive macro that lets drivers say that their type is safe to drop in atomic context.
Then require it for RcuBox and Fence

Alice Ryhl said:

We could define a new trait AtomicDrop with a derive macro that lets drivers say that their type is safe to drop in atomic context.

It'll be huge pain to do this if this isn't auto trait.
Eh, I don't think it's too bad. We can have a derive macro.
I guess whether A or B is good for Fence depends on the question whether we actually want to allow Fence::data::drop() to sleep

Well, I think the cost/benefit ratio is a not worth it for this case, given that you can still easily take a spinlock and drop something that is not AtomicDrop and there's no language mechanism to prevent that. Doing such trait require you to implement it for a lot of types in core and kernel, and just be able to use it for RcuBox feels an overkill.

DmaFence can be solved with SRCU, which I think isn't really an issue as DRM is already using SRCU in a few places.

I'd rather implement a auto-trait-like stuff in klint and use that instead, at least then I can still use it to do some best effort analysis for spinlock case. Klint already has some mechanisms in analyzing sleep behaviours; if we're not aiming for no-false-negative then it shouldn't be that tricky to implement something specifically for RcuBox.

If we can't prevent the driver from taking locks etc, then we could just as well use normal RCU

Reason being that you would then still rely on documentation: "Don't implement fence::data::drop() like this or like that"
Quick question, would an ARef<Something> in T, with Something::drop() being detected as sleepable be caught by this mechanism?
That logic has to depend on what Something is.
A trait could let each inner type declare whether its drop is sleepable or not.
You mean Arc? Both the trait approach or klint can detect that.

I'm of course worried about explicit ::drop() implementations doing something that's not allowed, but I'm even more worried about implicit drop()s on contained types, because those are the ones the user doesn't necessarily pay attention to

If the read-side critical section does not require to be sleepable, then SRCU is not the way to go. If we don't want the trait Alice proposed, we should either document it, or if sleepable Drop is a reasonable use-case, use queue_rcu_work(). Also, can't we catch violations with klint?

queue_rcu_work does not work, as it is really just call_rcu + queue_work. Only the RCU part orders with RCU barriers, and now you have the issue that you cannot guarantee the work to be complete when barrier completes in FenceCtx drop.
Oh well, if additionally the code relies on an rcu_barrier() then queue_rcu_work() does indeed not work; I thought it was exclusively about deferring drop() until all callbacks did complete.
That and doing something about the possiblilty that a DriverFence can drop in atomic context
Instead of the rcu_barrier()the code could wait for the RCU work though.
Not sure how would you implement that in FenceCtx; could be done but the complexity is growing even more now :)
In any case, we don't want an SRCU read-side critical section in a hot path where it is not required for the read-side.
How would the klint idea work, what would need to be done for that?
Well, I need to actually implement it :)

I mean, the rest of the kernel so far solves this through documentation + might_sleep() et al, warning on runtime.

If we need to get it water tight on compile time.. seems there is then a bit of work to be done

Danilo Krummrich said:

In any case, we don't want an SRCU read-side critical section in a hot path where it is not required for the read-side.

Which path is hot enough that you think the SRCU overhead (basically 1 per-cpu counter increment + 1 memory barrier) is too much?
Currently, we seem to have two users in-tree that have sleepable code in release(), so there are cases.
If we're stuck with normal RCU, then I think documenting that drop must not sleep is the first reasonable step.
But they do not seem unavoidable.
Submitting jobs to the GPU / accelerator is rather performance critical, srcu_read_lock() / srcu_read_unlock() add two smp_mb() which are avoidable.
When submitting jobs you have a DriverFence which doesn't need this, though? Only callbacks that cannot ascertain that DriverFence is not yet destroyed need to use SRCU to protect.
Could we maybe start with a compile-time FenceData::needs_drop() == false check?
With the FenceCtx design, I'd assume that most resources would be attached to the FenceCtx anyway
Need to check what @Daniel Almeida put in his FenceData
IIRC nothing, since we can derive the current syncobj value from the GPU itself, but apparently that is not the case for all drivers?

Gary Guo said:

When submitting jobs you have a DriverFence which doesn't need this, though? Only callbacks that cannot ascertain that DriverFence is not yet destroyed need to use SRCU to protect.

Fair enough, not a concern for me if it is not in a hot path.

IIRC nothing, since we can derive the current syncobj value from the GPU itself, but apparently that is not the case for all drivers?

Yep, I just checked those structs are empty indeed
OK. So we shall do SRCU and then the "only" thing we need to tell the driver is to be careful with taking locks in fence::data::drop()?
I mean, that's basically the same issue
@Boris Brezillon I asked a llm to do a research on the other drivers back then, to see if we could remove this FenceData thing completely, and it said no and pointed to amdgpu
All sorts of C drivers do container_of on their fence. That is their equivalent of Rust's data: T
yeah, but notion of context was lacking there
in your design, all callbacks basically go back to the context
in which you can store additional stuff

Anyways.

So then we enforce must_not_need_drop() == true and can remove the RCU code from DriverFence::drop(), aye?

I need to keep the rcu_barrier, though, since the fence context name parameters need to be guarded.

just to be clear, the real trouble come as soon as FenceData holds on external shared resources which might be dropped as part of the DriverFence::drop() if and only if the FenceData instance is the last owner

I need to keep the rcu_barrier, though, since the fence context name parameters need to be guarded.

Yep, you need it to make sure the context is not dropped before all callbacks have returned
or something along those lines (sorry, I just paged everything DmaFence related out)

Mhm, thinking about it, if we really prohibit anything that needs Drop(), then the driver couldn't even store anything allocated, like even a string for debugging, in their fences anymore

I feel that this might provoke people into using raw pointers as data. They don't need drop.

@Boris Brezillon

It would seem that the "drop in atomic context" problem goes even a few centimeters deeper than expected

If we now indeed drop DriverFenceData with call_rcu(), then the last reference to FenceCtx can also drop in atomic context.

It is illegal to do both rcu_barrier() and synchronize_rcu() in atomic context. So I think we can do neither in FenceCtx::drop().

For dma_fence itself, this is fine, since the call_rcu() already guarantees that there can be no UAF. However, you still need some synchronization to guard against module unload.

Do Rust kernel modules execute a rcu_barrier() before unloading?

So, first off, I wonder why the DriverFenceData::fctx needs to be an Arc<FenceCtx>. Can't we define that FenceCtx outlives any of the DriverFenceData is instantiated with lifetimes, and have fctx: &FenceCtx? If we go for this, this means that there's no 'DriverFenceData can release the last Arc<FenceCtx>" and thus we can't end up with FenceCtx being destroyed from the DriverFenceData::drop() path
@Daniel Almeida self-refential pin-init wip: https://github.com/nbdd0121/linux/tree/dev/selfref
@Gary Guo ok, assuming this solves the self-referential issue, how about the T: 'static bound in XArray?
What bound?
